preamble: The shift from the mid-back rounded vowel [o] to the high-back rounded vowel [u] is a standard example of vowel raising. It often occurs as part of a systemic chain shift where the entire vowel inventory moves 'upwards', or as a form of vowel reduction in unstressed syllables.
phoneticEffects: Vowel Raising, Great Vowel Shift, Vowel Reduction.

examples.note: Great Vowel Shift: The ME close-mid long vowel raised to become the modern high-back vowel.
Systematic raising of unstressed final o to u.
Historical raising of the long close-mid back vowel; standard Polish orthography 'ó' represents this historical [oː].

Parallel to the *eː > i change, all Cholan languages raised Proto-Mayan long *oː to short /u/. Together these two changes collapsed the vowel-length distinction in Cholan by merging the long mid vowels with the short high vowels. Documented in Ch'ol, Ch'orti', and Chontal through Kaufman and Norman's comparative Proto-Cholan work.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Kaufman, T. & Norman, W. (1984). An Outline of Proto-Cholan Phonology, Morphology and Vocabulary. In Munro Edmonson (ed.), Phoneticism in Mayan Hieroglyphic Writing.
context: In unstressed syllables in Catalan; high central vowel allophone appears in reduction contexts
allophones_note: Catalan vowel height shift in unstressed syllables
